questions.question: What is the main cost of a synchronous cross-shard query?
options: Latency and coordination often grow with fanout width and shard count.
It automatically becomes cheaper as more shards are added.
It removes any need for indexes or derived views.
It guarantees globally ordered pagination for free.
questions.explanation: Cross-shard reads usually pay in network fanout, merge work, and tail latency.

questions.question: What is the best first question when a product asks for a global query after sharding?
options: Whether the query must be exact and on the critical user path, or can be derived and eventual.
Whether the SQL keywords should be uppercase.
Whether the service logo needs updating.
Whether every shard uses the same hostname prefix.
questions.explanation: Freshness and latency expectations determine whether live fanout is even appropriate.

questions.question: When is bounded scatter-gather usually acceptable?
options: When query rate is modest, shard fanout is constrained, and the user path can tolerate the merge cost.
When every request in the system should hit every shard for consistency.
When the query needs sub-millisecond latency at global scale.
When the shard count is expected to grow without limit and no one wants observability.
questions.explanation: Low-rate, bounded fanout can be reasonable; unbounded critical-path fanout is not.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of a materialized global view?
options: It gives fast reads, but adds pipeline complexity, duplicate storage, and freshness lag.
It removes the need for schema management forever.
It makes all queries exact and strongly consistent with no delay.
It guarantees lower cost than every other approach.
questions.explanation: Precomputed views trade serving simplicity for pipeline and freshness complexity.

questions.question: Which metric best exposes a dangerous cross-shard read pattern?
options: Fanout width and merge latency rising on critical-path queries as shard count grows.
The number of screenshots attached to incident tickets.
The amount of whitespace in SQL files.
The average branch name length in Git.
questions.explanation: The query is dangerous when fanout and tail cost scale with topology.

questions.question: Why are naive offset-based pages often weak for global cross-shard ordering?
options: Because concurrent updates and distributed merge order can cause gaps or duplicates between pages.
Because offsets are only supported by NoSQL databases.
Because pagination is impossible after sharding.
Because every shard returns identical ordering automatically.
questions.explanation: Distributed merges need stable merge keys and continuation tokens, not simplistic offsets.

questions.question: When should a query move to a search or analytics backend?
options: When flexible, global filtering would otherwise overload primary shards or require large fanout routinely.
When the query is a single-key point lookup.
When the system wants fewer moving parts regardless of product need.
When freshness must always be exactly zero lag.
questions.explanation: Flexible global filtering is often better served by specialized derived systems.

questions.question: What makes a strong senior answer on cross-shard reads?
options: It scopes query classes, names their semantics, and matches each to local, derived, or bounded-fanout execution.
It says every important query can stay unchanged after sharding.
It avoids discussing latency or freshness trade-offs.
It recommends a search engine for every read pattern by default.
questions.explanation: Strong answers classify query types and choose execution strategies deliberately.
